text: the Lord for the truth of His Word and for giving us a Bible and for the opportunity that we have to hide God's Word in our hearts. Psalm 23 this evening, Psalm 23 this evening, and last week, actually a few weeks ago, we had the opportunity to study one of the other names of God, Jehovah Jireh, from Genesis chapter 22, and that name Jehovah Jireh, the Lord who provides. And last week we started dealing with Jehovah Ra, the Lord my Shepherd, and as we looked at that study last week, we really focused primarily on the fact of who God used to reveal to us as a human penman the name the Lord my Shepherd, Jehovah Ra, and that being David. And we talked about the significance of David himself being a shepherd and being able to easily relate to what was being communicated by God when he would use that name Jehovah Ra. And so now as we, I mentioned this last week, that we would spend several weeks looking at various aspects with regard to what does it mean, what is God communicating to us when he says that one of his names is Jehovah Ra, the Lord my Shepherd. Tonight we're gonna look at the first few verses here from Psalm 23 and consider this truth, my Shepherd provides for me, my Shepherd provides for me. As we begin to think about that, obviously there's a connection with Jehovah Jireh, the Lord who provides, and we look at that in Genesis 22, but I want you to notice here in Psalm 23, the first three verses, and again it's a psalm that many of us are familiar with, the Bible says, the Lord is my Shepherd, I shall not want. He maketh me to lie down in green pastures, he leadeth me beside the still waters, he restoreth my soul, he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake. We're gonna think about this evening that my Shepherd, the Lord is my Shepherd, and my Shepherd he provides for me, and I mentioned last week as we talked some about David and understanding shepherding and all the the responsibilities of a shepherd and all of the needs of the sheep, and we mentioned this, that David would be able to relate easily with what God was seeking to communicate because David understood how needy the sheep were, and then David as a shepherd himself understands that the Lord Jehovah, the self-existent one, that he is my Shepherd, and what a blessing to consider the truth of God's Word and understanding the truth that is being communicated. This idea tonight that the Lord my Shepherd, that he provides for me, we notice several things here. Notice in verse number two, the Bible says this, he maketh me to lie down in green pastures. I want you to think about just for a moment the imagery of this verse of Scriptures. We're gonna think about sheep and shepherd, and you think about the the natural truth of sheep and a shepherd, and the sheep have a need for nutrition, they have a need to eat, and it is in the best interest of the shepherd to provide well for the sheep, and he wants them because it's his livelihood, it's his occupation, and we find that he is going to leave them in here. As we begin to think about God and him being our shepherd, and we look at this imagery here, he maketh me to lie down in green pastures. I begin to understand this evening that physical, natural truth that is being communicated to us, but then as we understand the spiritual truth and how God, he takes care of our needs, he provides for our needs. The Bible doesn't say that he leadeth me to pastures. The Bible says that he maketh me to lie down in green pastures. If you know something about sheep, sheep are really kind of, you know, we use that word sheepish, right? I mean, they're kind of nervous animal, and they're always concerned about being eaten up by something, and the reality is that it says he maketh me to lie down in green pastures. We will notice in a next lesson about how my shepherd not only provides for me, but he protects me, and we see that even here in this opening verse, in verse number two, dealing with what our shepherd does for us, and it says in green pastures, then we find that it says he leadeth me beside the still waters. The still waters. It's my understanding, as I've done some outside research with regard to sheep and their behavior and what have you, that they do not have the capacity of being able to really drink from rushing waters, and so therefore, we find that, again, the Bible would give to us this truth that he leads me, he leadeth me beside the still waters. And you begin to understand, again, as David, being the human penman, communicating these things to us, he understood the importance as a shepherd of leading and providing for his sheep, and providing for them green pastures, and having the awareness of where am I going to lead the sheep that have been left to my keeping, where am I going to lead them, and to take them to still waters, because they need that nourishment. They need the sustenance. You know, God knows exactly what we need, and God takes good care of us. And I understand that as we look at this passage, and we begin to look at these phrases with regard to the green pastures and the still waters, and we notice the significance of it this evening, that God provides for me. My shepherd, he provides for me. We notice in verse number three, as we continue to understand this truth of provision, it says that he restoreth my soul. And the reality is that we begin to look at this phraseology, and the word restore there carries with it that idea of refreshing, and we see how that even from verse two, with those green pastures and the still waters, that it would be refreshing. And I begin to think about how God restores us, how he refreshes my soul. You know what a blessing it is to be nourished by God, to understand. And this evening, obviously, we're talking about some, yes, God provides for us physically, but we have to understand the spiritual provision of our God. The fact that God provides for us physically is obviously a blessing, and we ought to be thankful for it, and we ought to acknowledge it, but the reality is that God provides for our spiritual sustenance. He provides for us what we need, and no doubt as we get into God's word, as he gives to us the bread of life, that it restores our soul. It refreshes us. And what a blessing it is to understand that. The Bible then says that he leadeth me, notice at the end of verse number three, in the paths of righteousness for his namesake. Begin to think about the leadership that God provides for us, and I'm thankful this evening that we understand that God has given to us his word. Thy word is truth. We understand that, but he says that his word is like a lamp, it's like a light, it provides for us direction. Oftentimes, as we think about the Old Testament, we think about the leadership for the nation of Israel, and obviously as they left from Egypt, we have these two very distinct things that God reveals to us. A pillar of cloud by day, and a pillar of fire by night. Begin to think about how those two thoughts there that God gives to us, the truths of his leadership, how that would provide direction for the people of God. And Moses was simply following as God would have that pillar move. He'd follow, and of course the people would say, why'd you bring us here? And it's like, do you see? Like we're following God, and God is leading them, and he would use that pillar of cloud and that pillar of fire to provide direction. This evening, we don't have a pillar of cloud by day or a pillar of fire by night, but I believe, and I would argue, that we have something far superior because we have God's word, and we have the Holy Spirit of God who indwells us. The Spirit of God closed that door, and other times Paul says that there's an open door, and you see the leadership of the Spirit of God in the lives of his people, and what a blessing. Now, we understand tonight God's not going to lead us contrary. His Spirit's not going to lead you contrary to his word. There's going to be consistency. There's going to be a compliment as you begin to look at, you know, this is what God is leading me to do. Well, it's got to be consistent with what he's revealed to us within his word. And so this evening, when we think about that direction, and all of us need direction, and we spoke about this a little bit during the message of Jehovah Jireh, and we see it here in the text, and we understand this evening that God, my shepherd, Jehovah Ra, that he, my shepherd, he provides for me. So we're going to think about that this evening. I got some thoughts and questions for you as we look at this truth that my shepherd provides for me. Number one, if you're taking notes, do we recognize his provision? Do we recognize his provision? In your life and in my life, in our lives corporately as a church, do we recognize God's provision? And I believe this evening that it's important, yay, imperative that we discipline ourselves to recognize his provision in our lives. that we're careful to acknowledge that every good gift, every perfect gift, where does it come from? We find that this evening the psalmist would say that the Lord daily loadeth me with benefits and it's a discipline that we would recognize his provision in our lives. See, the danger is that we start thinking that we somehow have gotten whatever it is that we have. That somehow the provision that we enjoy, that it's the result of our own abilities, our own work. Now, the reality is that God has commanded us to work and God will bless that work through providing us provision but we have to acknowledge that it's God who gives us the ability to work. So ultimately, it's God who gives us the provision. It's God who gives you the ability to have the job that you have, to think the way that you're able to think, to do whatever it is that you're able to do and sometimes we have people say, well, you know, I was well-trained and I have good genes and smart parents and I have all of, no, no, no, listen. Normally, I say normally at times on various messages, I've simply referenced a verse of scripture from this text and we've continued and moved forward but I want you to see what God is saying to the children of Israel as they would get and prepare for entering into the land of promise. In Deuteronomy eight, beginning in verse number seven, notice what the Bible says. For the Lord thy God bringeth thee into a good land, a land of brooks of water, of fountains and depths that spring out of valleys and hills, a land of wheat and barley and vines and fig trees and pomegranates, a land of oil, olive and honey, a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness, thou shalt not lack anything in it. Isn't that wonderful? It says a land whose stones are iron and out of whose hills thou mayest dig brass. Now, you ever see a sign that says beware? What do you do when you see a sign that says beware? Do you ignore it or do you take note? See, I'm the kind of guy, if it says beware of dogs, listen, I am taking note, I am not ignoring it, I am believing whoever put the sign up that there are dogs, why? Because I don't get along well with dogs, and so therefore, I am taking note to the beware sign. Now, I know growing up, my grandparents, they had dogs, and their dogs died when I was a young kid, and for probably eight or 10 years, they kept signs up on their gates that said beware of dog, but there are no dogs there anymore. But you know, people still took note of those signs, and so when God uses the word beware, he's trying to get our attention, right? Listen, there's a warning that God has given us, notice, beware that thou forget not the Lord thy God, and not keeping his commandments and his judgments and his statutes which I command thee this day, lest when thou hast eaten and art full and hast built goodly houses and dwelt therein, and when thy hands and thy flocks multiply, and thy silver and thy gold is multiplied, and all that thou hast is multiplied, verse 14, then thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the Lord thy God which brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt from the house of bondage, who led thee through that great and terrible wilderness, wherein were fiery serpents and scorpions and drought, where there was no water, who brought thee forth water out of the rock of Flint, who fed thee in the wilderness with manna, which thy fathers knew not, that he might humble thee, and that he might prove thee to do thee good at thy latter end, and thou say in thine heart, notice what he tells him, here's the warning, and thou say in thine heart, my power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth.
